courses.title: Engineering Mathematics I
courses.summary: Single-variable calculus rebuilt as a working tool: limits, derivatives, integrals, and first differential equations, always pointed at rates, areas, and physical balances you will meet again in thermodynamics and vibrations.

lessons.scope: What a function is when its argument is a temperature or a flow rate. Unit discipline and dimensional homogeneity as the first error-check every engineer owns. Orders of magnitude and sanity estimates.
preview: Why must every term of a physical equation share the same dimensions, and what error does this catch first?
A pump's flow is 'about 100' — cubic metres per hour or per day? Show how an order-of-magnitude check settles it.

video.title: Treating Units Algebraically and Dimensional Analysis

lessons.scope: The limit as the formal version of 'what happens as we approach'. Continuity and why physical quantities are usually, but not always, continuous. One-sided limits at switching events.
preview: Give a plant quantity that genuinely jumps discontinuously, and one that only appears to.
What does a one-sided limit describe at a relay switching instant?

video.title: Lec 2 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Limits

lessons.scope: Definition from first principles, then the derivative as velocity, heat-up rate, and wear rate. Differentiability versus continuity. Reading slopes off real trend data.
preview: A bearing temperature trend rises 2 °C/hour: what derivative statement is that, and toward what does it point?
Why can a function be continuous yet not differentiable at a point — and what would that look like on a trend?

video.title: Lec 1 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Rate of Change

lessons.t: Differentiation rules and the chain rule
lessons.scope: Product, quotient, and chain rules with mechanical fluency as the goal. Implicit differentiation. The chain rule as the engine behind every related-rates problem in plant contexts.
preview: Tank level depends on flow, flow on valve position: which rule chains the sensitivities together?
Differentiate x·e^x and explain which rule did the work.

video.title: Lec 4 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Chain Rule

lessons.scope: e and ln as the natural language of charging, cooling, and decay processes. Log scales for spanning decades of data. Semilog reading of exponential trends — the shape behind Newton cooling and RC circuits.
preview: Why do cooling curves plot straight on semilog axes, and what does the slope encode?
What does a time constant of 5 minutes say about how long until a sensor reads ~63% of a step change?

video.title: Lec 6 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Exponential and Log

lessons.scope: First and second derivative tests. Framing a physical question as minimize-cost or maximize-output with constraints stated honestly. Classic geometry-of-a-tank optimizations as rehearsal for design trade-offs.
preview: A closed tank must hold fixed volume with minimum surface: set up (don't solve) the optimization.
What distinguishes a local maximum from the global one on a cost curve, and why does the difference matter?

video.title: Lec 11 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Max-min Problems

lessons.scope: Riemann sums to the definite integral. Accumulated flow from a rate curve, energy from a power trace. The Fundamental Theorem as the bridge between rates and totals.
preview: Power drawn over a shift is a curve: what integral gives energy consumed, and in what units?
State the Fundamental Theorem in one sentence about rates and totals.

video.title: Lec 18 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Definite Integrals

lessons.scope: Substitution, integration by parts, and simple partial fractions. Choosing a technique by recognizing structure. Numerical fallback when no closed form exists.
preview: Which technique handles ∫x·e^x dx, and why does substitution alone fail?
When is a numerical integral the honest choice over an analytic one?

video.title: Lec 30 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Integration by Parts

lessons.t: Applications: areas, volumes, work, and averages
lessons.scope: Solids of revolution for vessels and shafts. Work integrals for pumping and lifting. Mean value of a fluctuating signal — the first meeting with RMS thinking.
preview: Why does RMS, not the plain mean, represent the heating effect of a fluctuating current?
Set up the work integral for pumping a tank's contents up 10 m — what varies inside the integral?

video.title: Lec 23 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Work and Average Value

lessons.scope: Separable and linear first-order ODEs. Tank mixing, Newton cooling, and first-order sensor lag solved end to end. Time constants as the physical meaning of the solution.
preview: Newton cooling: write the ODE and say what each symbol claims physically.
Two mixing tanks differ only in volume: which has the longer time constant, and why?

video.title: Lec 16 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Differential Equations

lessons.scope: Linear and quadratic approximation of functions near an operating point. Where sin θ ≈ θ comes from and when it lies. Error terms as an engineering honesty clause.
preview: Where does sin θ ≈ θ come from, and at roughly what angle does the lie exceed 1%?
Why does linearization around an operating point underpin control engineering?

video.title: Lec 38 | MIT 18.01 Single Variable Calculus, Fall 2007 — Taylor's Series

courses.career:
Calculus literacy is not a math credential on this platform — it is the analytical engine behind roles across every industrial sector. Three sector snapshots for this course:

  • Construction & real estate — facilities and HVAC engineering for large mixed-use developments (in Kuwait, think of an operator like Mabanee and the major contracting firms behind such projects): cooling-load profiles, energy-consumption trends, and chiller-plant optimization are rates, accumulations, and optima — this course's material wearing a hard hat.
  • FMCG & food processing — process and utilities engineers at dairy and beverage producers such as KDD live on exactly these tools: pasteurizer temperature transients (first-order responses), CIP cycle optimization, and steam and refrigeration demand curves integrated into daily energy accounts.
  • Global technology & OEM — application and service engineers at industrial-technology houses such as Siemens turn derivative-and-integral fluency into drive-sizing studies, pump and fan affinity-law energy cases, and the analytics behind condition-monitoring platforms.

Interview angle: every one of these employers tests whether you can turn a trend into a decision — "consumption is rising: how fast, since when, and when does it cross the limit?" is a calculus question asked in business clothes. The worked problems in this course are rehearsals for precisely that conversation.

courses.title: Engineering Physics I — Mechanics
courses.summary: Classical mechanics built from scratch for engineers: units and vectors, motion, Newton's laws, energy, momentum, rotation, and first oscillations — the physical foundation under statics, dynamics, thermodynamics, and vibrations.

lessons.scope: Physical quantities and SI base units; significant figures and estimation discipline. Vectors made operational: components, addition, dot and cross products in physical language. The toolkit every mechanics course after this one assumes.
preview: Decompose a 100 N force at 37° above horizontal into components — the operation statics repeats daily.
Why is torque a vector while temperature is not?

video.title: Dr. Melnick — Units and Significant Figures

lessons.scope: Position, velocity, and acceleration defined as derivatives of motion, hand in hand with the calculus course. Motion graphs read fluently: slopes and areas carrying physical meaning. The constant-acceleration equations derived, not memorized.
preview: A hoist accelerates from rest at 0.5 m/s²: how far does it travel in 6 s? (9 m)
What does the area under a velocity–time graph mean physically?

video.title: Dynamics — Particle Kinematics Notes

lessons.scope: Projectile motion as two independent one-dimensional motions running on a shared clock. Relative velocity and moving reference frames. Where 2-D kinematics lives in machinery: belt speeds, transfer trajectories, parts leaving conveyors.
preview: A part leaves a horizontal conveyor at 2 m/s from 1.25 m height: how far out does it land? (≈1.0 m)
Why don't the horizontal and vertical motions of a projectile interfere?

video.title: Dynamics — 2D Rectangular Motion Notes

lessons.scope: The three laws stated precisely and read as engineering: equilibrium, F = ma, and action–reaction pairs that never act on the same body. Mass distinguished from weight for good. Free-body diagrams as the discipline statics will industrialize.
preview: What is the weight of a 50 kg motor, and why is 'kg' not a force? (≈490 N)
Why is a free-body diagram drawn for one body at a time?

video.title: Dr. Melnick — Newton's 3 Laws of Motion

lessons.scope: Friction, static and kinetic, with its adaptive character; inclined planes; tension and pulleys. Multi-body problems solved with paired free-body diagrams. The physics behind holding brakes, belt slip, and why inclined conveyors have a maximum angle.
preview: A crate sits on a 20° incline with μs = 0.30: does it stay? (No — tan 20° = 0.36 > 0.30)
Why does static friction adjust to the load while kinetic friction does not?

video.title: Dynamics — Particle Kinetics Notes

lessons.scope: Work as force through distance, with the sign conventions that matter. Kinetic energy and the work–energy theorem derived from F = ma. Power as the rate of doing work — the bridge to every kilowatt on a nameplate.
preview: How much work does it take to raise a 200 kg load 12 m against gravity? (≈23.5 kJ)
What computation does the work–energy theorem let you skip entirely?

video.title: Dynamics — Work and Energy Notes, Part 1

lessons.scope: Conservative forces and potential energy; mechanical energy conservation, and exactly where friction breaks it. Energy accounting as the engineer's first audit tool — losses always go somewhere, and the somewhere is billable.
preview: From what height must a 2 kg part fall to hit 7 m/s? (≈2.5 m)
When is mechanical energy not conserved, and where does the difference go?

video.title: Dynamics — Conservation of Energy Notes

lessons.scope: The impulse–momentum theorem; conservation of momentum where external forces vanish; elastic and inelastic collisions distinguished by what happens to kinetic energy. Impact loading: why dropped loads and hammer blows produce forces far beyond their weights.
preview: Two 5 kg carts collide and stick, one moving at 4 m/s: final speed? (2 m/s)
Why does extending the stopping time reduce the impact force?

video.title: Dynamics — Conservation of Momentum Notes

lessons.scope: Angular position, velocity, and acceleration; the rolling connection v = ωr. Torque defined properly and computed about real axes; moment of inertia as rotational mass. RPM fluency — the native unit of the machine floor.
preview: Convert 1450 rpm to rad/s. (≈152)
Why does the same force produce different torques at different radii?

video.title: Dynamics — Rigid Body Kinematics: Linear vs Angular Velocity

lessons.t: Rotation II: dynamics and angular momentum
lessons.scope: Newton's second law for rotation, τ = Iα, with rotational work and kinetic energy. Angular momentum and its conservation. Flywheels as kinetic-energy banks — the physics the machinery course will engineer into presses and compressors.
preview: Why does a flywheel's stored energy grow with the square of its speed?
A wheel spins down under constant friction torque: what does the linear speed-time trace look like?

lessons.scope: Simple harmonic motion: the spring–mass system and the pendulum, with period formulas derived from F = ma. Natural frequency as a machine's fingerprint — the seed the vibrations course will grow. Waves in outline: propagation, frequency, wavelength.
preview: A 2 kg mass hangs on an 800 N/m spring: natural frequency? (≈3.2 Hz)
Why does a pendulum's period not care about its mass?

courses.title: Engineering Drawing & CAD
courses.summary: The plant's native written language: orthographic projection, sections, dimensions and tolerances, weld and surface symbols — read first, draw second — plus enough parametric CAD to model and detail real parts.
taught_from: MIT OCW / NPTEL: Engineering Drawing and Computer Graphics (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: The drawing as a contract between design, fabrication, and maintenance. Title blocks, revisions, and drawing control. What 'as-built' means and why it is often untrue.
preview: Who is protected by drawing revision control, and what goes wrong without it?
Why is an 'as-built' stamp often untrue, and what does that mean for the maintenance engineer using it?

lessons.scope: First-angle and third-angle projection and how to tell them apart on sight. Choosing principal views. Reading a three-view drawing back into a 3-D object.
preview: Given the projection symbol, how do you tell first-angle from third-angle at a glance?
Why might two correct views still be ambiguous, and what resolves it?

lessons.scope: Isometric drawing for communication with the shop floor. Sketching a part freehand in proportion. When a pictorial view misleads and a section is owed instead.
preview: When does an isometric view mislead about a feature, and which view type fixes it?
Why sketch in proportion even when CAD exists?

lessons.scope: Full, half, offset, and revolved sections. Hatching conventions by material. The conventions for ribs, shafts, and fasteners that are deliberately not sectioned.
preview: Why are ribs and fasteners conventionally not hatched in section?
Choose a section type for an offset bolt circle on a flange — and defend it.

lessons.scope: Datum thinking: dimension from function, not convenience. Chain versus baseline dimensioning and tolerance stack-up. Over-dimensioning as an error, not a courtesy.
preview: Chain versus baseline dimensioning: which accumulates tolerance, and why?
What makes an over-dimensioned drawing an error rather than extra information?

lessons.scope: Limits and fits for shafts and holes; clearance, transition, and interference classes. Why a bearing seat tolerance is a maintenance issue, not a design nicety. Reading fit callouts on real drawings.
preview: An H7/g6 callout: clearance, transition, or interference — and what duty suits it?
Why is a bearing-seat fit a maintenance concern rather than a design nicety?

lessons.scope: Feature control frames, datums, flatness, position, runout. What GD&T buys over coordinate tolerances. Runout callouts as the language of rotating-equipment repair standards.
preview: What does total runout control that circular runout does not?
Why does GD&T's datum system beat coordinate tolerancing for function?

video.title: Understanding GD&T

lessons.scope: Thread designation and fastener callouts. Weld symbols on structural drawings — the arrow side convention that everyone gets wrong once. Surface finish symbols and Ra values.
preview: On a weld symbol, what changes between arrow-side and other-side placement?
Decode M16×2: what do the numbers promise the fitter?

lessons.scope: Sketch–constrain–feature workflow in any parametric CAD tool. Design intent: modeling so the part edits sanely. Fully defined sketches as a discipline.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Engineering Drawing and Computer Graphics
preview: What is design intent in a parametric model, and how does a bad sketch betray it?
Why should sketches be fully defined before features are built?

lessons.scope: Mates and degrees of freedom in assemblies. Generating dimensioned drawings from models. Bills of materials and balloon callouts — the bridge to spare-parts thinking.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Engineering Drawing and Computer Graphics
preview: What do assembly mates remove, and what should remain free?
Why do balloons and BOM line numbers matter to a storeroom?

lessons.scope: GA drawings, fabrication drawings, and datasheets read side by side. Tracing a dimension from a pump GA to a foundation bolt plan. Practice on structural-steel and equipment drawings.
preview: Trace a dimension from a pump GA to the foundation bolt plan: what drawings intervene?
What three things do you check first when handed an unfamiliar GA?

courses.title: Engineering Statics
courses.summary: Equilibrium as a habit of mind: forces, moments, free-body diagrams, trusses and frames, friction, and centroids — the grammar under every strength-of-materials and machine-design calculation that follows.

lessons.scope: Force as a vector; components and resultants in 2-D and 3-D. Units and the weight–mass distinction that still catches graduates. Resolving cable and strut forces.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Why is weight not mass, and where does the confusion bite in bolt-load calculations?
Resolve a 10 kN cable force at 30° to horizontal into components.

video.title: Statics: Lesson 4 - Cartesian Notation & Vector Addition Explained!

lessons.scope: Moment of a force about a point and an axis. Couples as pure turning effect. Varignon's theorem used to simplify real load cases.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: What does a couple do that a single force cannot?
State Varignon's theorem and one situation where it saves work.

video.title: Statics: Lesson 21 - Introduction to Moments: The r x F Method

lessons.scope: The single most important skill in this course: isolate, replace, label. Support types and their reactions. Every equilibrium error traces back to a bad FBD.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Name the three steps of a free-body diagram and the most common omission.
What reactions does a fixed support supply that a pin does not?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 18 - How to Solve Complex Systems with Multiple FBDs

lessons.scope: Concurrent force systems in 2-D and 3-D. Cable-and-pulley systems and spring balances. Solving cleanly with components versus geometry.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: A particle under three concurrent forces is in equilibrium: what geometric fact must the forces obey?
When do you solve by components versus by force triangle?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 16 - 2D Force Equilibrium & Frictionless Pulleys

lessons.scope: The full ΣF = 0, ΣM = 0 toolkit. Two- and three-force members as shortcuts. Statically determinate versus indeterminate — and why the distinction matters later.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Why does a two-force member carry load only along its axis?
Statically determinate versus indeterminate: what changes in how you must solve?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 29 - 2D Reaction at Supports, Example Problem

lessons.scope: Method of joints and method of sections. Zero-force members by inspection. Reading a pipe-rack or roof truss as a load path, not a picture.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: How do you spot a zero-force member by inspection?
Method of sections: when does it beat method of joints, and why?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 48 - Trusses, Method of Joints

lessons.scope: Multi-member structures with internal pins. Dismembering frames correctly — action and reaction at every pin. Mechanical advantage of linkages and clamps.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Why must frames be dismembered at pins with action–reaction pairs?
What is mechanical advantage in a toggle clamp, statically speaking?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 53 - Frame Problem with Two-Force Members

lessons.t: Internal forces: shear and bending moment
lessons.scope: Cutting a beam and exposing N, V, M. Shear and moment diagrams for point and distributed loads. The diagrams as the input strength-of-materials will demand.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: What sign convention makes shear and moment diagrams consistent?
Where is bending moment maximum when shear crosses zero — and why?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 57 - Introduction to Internal Forces, M N V

lessons.scope: Dry friction, impending motion, and the friction cone. Wedges, screws, and belt friction — the capstan equation derived. Why belt tension ratios govern conveyor drives.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Derive the direction of impending motion's friction force on a wedge being driven.
Why does belt tension ratio grow exponentially with wrap angle?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 62 - Friction is Fun, Box on an Incline

lessons.scope: Centroids by integration and by composite parts. Center of gravity of built-up sections and equipment skids. Lifting-plan relevance: where the hook must sit.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: Why does a lifting hook need to sit over the center of gravity, and what happens if it doesn't?
Composite centroid: outline the tabular method for an L-section.

video.title: Statics: Lesson 37 - Finding the Center of Gravity & Centroids

lessons.scope: Second moment of area by integration and parallel-axis theorem. Composite sections used in structural tables. The property that will set bending stress in Strength of Materials.
lessons.src: MIT OCW 2.001; video: Jeff Hanson (Texas Tech)
preview: What does the parallel-axis theorem add, and when must you use it?
Why does an I-beam's shape maximize second moment for its area?

video.title: Statics: Lesson 67 - Introduction to Area Moment of Inertia

courses.title: Materials Science I
courses.summary: Atoms to microstructure: bonding, crystal structures, defects, diffusion, and mechanical behavior — the vocabulary in which every later conversation about steels, failure, and heat treatment is conducted.
taught_from: Callister & Rethwisch, Materials Science and Engineering: An Introduction (Wiley)

lessons.scope: Materials classes — metals, ceramics, polymers, composites — and what each trades away. Structure–property–processing–performance as the course's spine. Reading a failed component as a materials question.
preview: Rank metals, ceramics, polymers by stiffness and by toughness — what trades where?
State the structure–property–processing–performance chain for one familiar part.

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 1 — Introduction to Solid-State Chemistry

lessons.scope: Metallic, ionic, covalent, and secondary bonding. Bond energy curves and where stiffness and thermal expansion come from. Why metals conduct and deform while ceramics shatter.
preview: Why do metals conduct and deform while ceramics insulate and shatter — from bonding alone?
What does a deep, narrow bond-energy well predict about stiffness and expansion?

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 9 — Lewis Structures I (bonding)

lessons.scope: BCC, FCC, and HCP cells; coordination and packing factors. Density calculations from lattice data. Why FCC metals form and slip differently from BCC — a fact with cold-weather consequences.
preview: Compute-style: which is denser at equal atomic mass, FCC or BCC, and why?
Why do BCC steels turn brittle in cold weather while FCC metals don't?

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 18 — Introduction to Crystallography

lessons.scope: Miller indices for directions and planes. Close-packed planes and slip systems. Anisotropy: why rolled plate has direction-dependent properties.
preview: What makes close-packed planes the natural slip planes?
Why does rolled plate have direction-dependent properties?

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 19 — Crystallographic Notation

lessons.scope: Vacancies, interstitials, and substitutional atoms; dislocations edge and screw; grain boundaries. The defect population as the true design variable. Perfect crystals are a textbook fiction — usefully so.
preview: Why are dislocations, not perfect lattices, the reason metals deform at realistic stresses?
Name the point, line, and planar defects and one property each controls.

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 23 — Point and Line Defects I

lessons.scope: Fick's first and second laws with the physical picture of jumping atoms. Temperature dependence through the Arrhenius form. Carburizing depth as a worked application — the √Dt scaling that recurs everywhere.
preview: Why does case-hardening depth scale with √(Dt)?
What does the Arrhenius form say about doubling temperature versus doubling time?

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 35 — Diffusion I

lessons.t: Mechanical properties I: elasticity and the tension test
lessons.scope: Engineering stress and strain; modulus, yield, UTS, ductility from the tensile curve. True versus engineering quantities. The tension test as the industry's reference experiment.
preview: From a tensile curve, extract modulus, yield, UTS, and ductility — where does each live?
Why does true stress keep rising while engineering stress falls after necking?

video.title: An Introduction to Stress and Strain

lessons.t: Mechanical properties II: hardness and toughness
lessons.scope: Hardness scales — Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell — and their conversions and abuses. Impact energy and the ductile-brittle transition. Hardness as the maintenance engineer's field instrument.
preview: Why is hardness a proxy for strength, and when does the proxy break?
What does the ductile–brittle transition mean for winter impact loads?

video.title: Understanding Material Strength, Ductility and Toughness

lessons.scope: Slip as dislocation motion. Strengthening by grain refinement, solid solution, strain hardening, and precipitates — all as obstacles to dislocations. Why 'harder' usually costs 'tougher'.
preview: Explain grain-boundary strengthening in one sentence about dislocations.
Why does strain hardening raise strength but cost ductility?

video.title: MIT 3.091 Lec 24 — Point and Line Defects II (dislocations)

lessons.scope: What annealing actually does to a cold-worked metal. Recrystallization temperature and its rule-of-thumb relation to melting point. The hot-working/cold-working boundary defined properly.
preview: What does recrystallization undo, and what does its temperature roughly track?
Define hot working without mentioning absolute temperature.

lessons.scope: Nucleation and growth; chill, columnar, and equiaxed zones in a casting. Segregation at the scale of dendrites and of ingots. The materials-science half of the casting lectures in MFG 105.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 9; Flemings, Solidification Processing
preview: Why do castings show chill, columnar, and equiaxed zones?
Where does microsegregation live, and why does it survive solidification?

courses.title: Engineering Computing
courses.summary: Python as a plant engineer's calculator and data tool: scripts, arrays, plots, curve fits, and small simulations — enough computing to automate the analyses the rest of this curriculum assigns.
taught_from: MIT OCW 6.0001 Introduction to Computer Science and Programming in Python (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: The spreadsheet's limits and the script's advantages: repeatability, audit trail, scale. Setting up Python and running a first calculation. Version thinking for engineering work.
preview: Name two failure modes of spreadsheet engineering that scripts avoid.
What makes a calculation reproducible?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 1 — What is Computation?

lessons.scope: Numbers, strings, booleans; assignment and naming that reads like engineering. Floating point and its surprises in unit conversions. Writing formulas as code without transcription errors.
preview: Why does 0.1 + 0.2 ≠ 0.3 in floating point, and when does an engineer care?
Show a unit conversion written so the units audit themselves.

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 3 — Strings, Guess-and-Check, Approximations

lessons.scope: Conditionals and loops for case logic and iteration. Guard clauses for invalid inputs. Translating a calculation procedure into control flow.
preview: Turn 'if the reading exceeds limit, flag it' into control flow — what guards the invalid-input case?
When is a while loop honest and a for loop not?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 2 — Branching and Iteration

lessons.scope: Functions as named, testable calculations. Arguments, returns, and docstrings that state units. Building a small library of personal engineering functions.
preview: What belongs in a function's docstring for an engineering calculation?
Why do small, single-purpose functions beat one long script?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 4 — Decomposition, Abstraction, and Functions

lessons.scope: Assertions and test cases that pin a calculation to a hand-checked answer before it is trusted at scale. Exceptions and guard clauses for bad inputs; unit sanity checks as an executable habit. Debugging as hypothesis testing, not stare-and-hope.
preview: What does an assertion buy you that a printed answer doesn't?
Why should a calculation function be tested against a hand-checked case before scale use?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 7 — Testing, Debugging, Exceptions, and Assertions

lessons.scope: Collections for measurement sets and equipment records. Reading and writing CSV files — the lingua franca of plant data exports. Cleaning real, messy data defensively.
preview: Why is CSV the lingua franca of plant data, and what are its traps?
Dict versus list for an equipment register: which and why?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 5 — Tuples, Lists, Aliasing, Mutability, and Cloning

lessons.scope: NumPy arrays for vectorized calculation over whole datasets. Elementwise operations, aggregation, and broadcasting. A vibration record processed without a single explicit loop.
preview: What does vectorization replace, and why is it faster and clearer?
Compute the RMS of an array in one line — conceptually, what happened?

video.title: MIT 6.0001 Lec 10 — Understanding Program Efficiency (best fit: why vectorized code wins)

lessons.scope: Time series, scatter, and log-scale plots with honest axes and units. The plot as an argument, not a decoration. Reproducing a textbook figure from raw numbers.
preview: Name three properties of an honest engineering plot.
When does a log axis reveal what a linear one hides?

video.title: MIT 6.0002 Lec 5 — Random Walks (best fit: building and reading plots)

lessons.scope: Least-squares fits to calibration and performance data. Interpolation for table lookups — steam tables and pump curves as targets. Residuals as the fit's honesty check.
preview: What do residuals tell you that R² alone does not?
Interpolation versus extrapolation: why is one routine and the other risky?

video.title: MIT 6.0002 Lec 9 — Understanding Experimental Data (curve fitting)

lessons.scope: Time-stepping a first-order ODE: tank level, cooling, simple reliability decay. Step size and stability observed experimentally. Simulation as a cheap laboratory.
preview: Time-step a cooling ODE: what goes wrong when the step is too large?
Why is simulation a cheap laboratory — and when is it a lying one?

video.title: MIT 6.0002 Lec 6 — Monte Carlo Simulation

lessons.scope: End-to-end exercise: load a work-order export, clean it, compute simple KPIs, and plot the story. The habits of reproducible analysis. This project pattern returns in Reliability Engineering.
preview: From a work-order export, which cleaning steps must precede any KPI?
Why should analysis scripts read raw data and never edit it?

video.title: MIT 6.0002 Lec 15 — Statistical Sins and Wrap Up (honest analysis habits)
